claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child is 20, which is 20 or over

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to ask you some questions about your child (or children).

**First, could you tell me about the age of the child you're claiming for?**

The eligibility rules depend on how old the child is:
- **Under 16**: Generally qualifies for Child Benefit
- **16 to 19**: May qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally doesn't qualify

Please tell me:
1. How many children are you looking to claim for?
2. What are their ages (and names if you'd like to share)?

This will help me guide you through the correct eligibility path.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for your help! I'm looking to claim for my two children - Alex who is 8, and Blake who is 20.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me help you assess eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me work through each child:

**For Alex (age 8):**
Since Alex is under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification category. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For Alex (age 8), I need to confirm: **Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

This is typically straightforward for an 8-year-old, but I need to confirm to proceed with the assessment.

**For Blake (age 20):**
Unfortunately, Blake is 20 years old, which means they are over the age limit for Child Benefit. Child Benefit generally only applies to children under 16, or those aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Once a young person turns 20, they no longer qualify for Child Benefit.

Let me know about Alex's work status, and then I can continue with the full assessment for Alex!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, I see. No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 8, still in primary school!

And yes, I understand about Blake being too old now. That makes sense.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming! Let me continue with Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question about Alex:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This would include benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed in Alex's own name (not as part of your family claim). 

For an 8-year-old in primary school, this is typically not applicable, but I need to confirm to continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name. They're just a regular 8-year-old!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue checking the eligibility criteria for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question about Alex:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm. Apprenticeships typically apply to older teenagers and young adults.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're only 8 and in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move on to questions about **you as the claimant**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. **Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of these apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common path)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK, or does one of these other situations apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't actually live in the UK. I live abroad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since you live abroad, let me check if you might qualify under special circumstances.

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant** (working for the UK government overseas, such as in the armed forces, civil service, or diplomatic service)? **Or are you living in certain qualifying countries** where UK Child Benefit can still be claimed?

If neither of these applies to you, then unfortunately you would not meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit, as it generally requires you to be normally living in the UK or to fall under one of these special categories.

Could you let me know:
1. Are you a Crown servant working abroad for the UK government?
2. If not, which country are you currently living in? (Some countries may have special arrangements)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad as a regular person - not working for the UK government.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that information. Let me navigate to the outcome based on your residency situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have enough information to provide you with the eligibility assessment for your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Final Results

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake. Here's the breakdown for each child:

---

### **Alex (age 8) - NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

While Alex meets all the child-related requirements:
- ✓ Under 16 years old
- ✓ Not working full-time
- ✓ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✓ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship

**The issue is with your residency status as the claimant.** Child Benefit requires that you (the person claiming) must:
- Normally live in the UK, OR
- Meet special circumstances such as:
  - Being a Crown servant working abroad
  - Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
  - Living in certain qualifying countries

Since you live abroad as a regular person and don't fall into any of these special categories, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.

---

### **Blake (age 20) - NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

Blake is **over the age limit** for Child Benefit. The benefit only applies to:
- Children under 16, OR
- Young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training

Once a young person turns 20, they no longer qualify for Child Benefit regardless of their circumstances.

---

### **What This Means:**

Unfortunately, Child Benefit is generally only available to claimants who are resident in the UK. Even though Alex would otherwise qualify as a child, the residency requirement applies to you as the claimant.

### **What You Could Consider:**

- If your circumstances change and you move back to the UK to live permanently, you could then apply for Child Benefit for Alex
- If you have any connection to EU Settlement Scheme status or other special circumstances in the future, this might change your eligibility

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions about these rules or your specific situation, please let me know.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and meets all the child-related requirements: under 16, not working full-time, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. Child Benefit requires the claimant to normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances such as being a Crown servant abroad, having settled/pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or living in certain qualifying countries. Since the claimant lives abroad as a regular person and does not fall into any of these special categories, they are ineligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old, which exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or those aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Once a young person turns 20, they no longer qualify regardless of their circumstances. Additionally, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements, which would also prevent eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is seeking to claim Child Benefit for two children: Alex (8 years old) and Blake (20 years old). Blake is ineligible due to being over the age limit (20 years old). While Alex meets all child-related eligibility criteria, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements as they live abroad and are not a Crown servant or in any other qualifying category. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.
